items.text: Energy levels / energy shells — designated K, L, M, N (or 1,2,3,4...)
Neither gains nor loses energy → called stationary orbits
Absorbs energy (lower → higher orbit)
Emits energy (higher → lower orbit)
13.6 eV required to remove electron from ground state of hydrogen
n₁=1, n₂=2,3,4... (UV region)
n₁=2, n₂=3,4,5... (visible region)
n₁=3, n₂=4,5,6... (IR region)
Splitting of spectral line in strong magnetic field
Splitting of spectral line in strong electric field

rows: Size & energy of orbit; max e⁻ = 2n²; total orbitals = n²
Shape of orbital; no. of subshells = n
Direction of spin; max 2 e⁻ per orbital

items.text: No two electrons in an atom can have all four quantum numbers same
In degenerate orbitals, pairing occurs only after each orbital is singly occupied
s = spherical, p = dumbbell, d = double dumbbell
1, 3, 5, 7 orbitals respectively (= 2l+1)
2, 6, 10, 14 electrons respectively (= 2(2l+1))

items.latex: \hat{H}\psi = E\psi \quad\text{(atomic orbital = region where }\psi^2\text{ is maximum)}

items.text: Differentiating e⁻ enters s-orbital. Groups 1 & 2. Config: ns¹⁻²
Differentiating e⁻ enters p-orbital. 6 groups. Config: ns² np¹⁻⁶. Contains all non-metals, metalloids and few metals
Differentiating e⁻ enters (n-1)d sub-shell. 10 groups. Config: (n-1)d¹⁻¹⁰ ns¹⁻²
Differentiating e⁻ enters (n-2)f sub-shell. Config: (n-2)f¹⁻¹⁴(n-1)d⁰⁻¹ ns²
Two outermost sub-shells incompletely filled. Zn, Cd, Hg are d-block but NOT transition elements (fully filled d¹⁰)
Covalent radius (non-metals), metallic/crystal radius (metals), van der Waals radius
Atomic radius increases (new sub-shell added; more screening)
Atomic radius decreases (same sub-shell, nuclear charge increases)
In lanthanides, 4f electrons have poor shielding → steady decrease in size. Consequences: Zr≈Hf, Nb≈Ta, Mo≈W in size; separation of lanthanides difficult
Minimum energy to remove most loosely bound electron from neutral gaseous atom. Unit: kJ/mol or eV
IE decreases (atomic size and screening increase)
IE generally increases (atomic size decreases, nuclear charge increases)

items.latex: Li < B < Be < C < O < N < F < Ne
Na < Al < Mg < Si < S < P < Cl < Ar

items.text: Half-filled (N, P) and completely-filled (Be, Mg) subshells are extra stable → higher IE than expected
Helium. Highest IE group: noble gases (Group 0/18)
Na has 3s¹ — readily loses one electron to get noble gas config
Na⁺ has noble gas config → very hard to remove another electron
Energy released when electron is added to neutral gaseous atom. A(g) + e⁻ → A⁻(g)
EA decreases (atomic size increases)
EA increases (atomic size decreases)
Chlorine (not F) — F has small size causing strong inter-electronic repulsion
Relative tendency of bonded atom to attract shared electron pair. No units. F = 4.0 (highest)
Li≈Mg, Be≈Al, B≈Si — due to similar atomic size, electronegativity and polarizing power
Atom with 8 electrons in valence shell is most stable
Strong electrostatic force between oppositely charged ions
Large size, low charge, low IP are favourable
Small size, low charge, high EA are favourable
Both electrons in shared pair donated by one atom (donor). Eg: NH₄⁺ formation
For given cation: covalent character increases with size of anion. Eg: KI > KF in covalency
Covalent character increases with higher charges on cation or anion. Eg: SnCl₄ > SnCl₂
For given anion: covalent character increases with decrease in cation size. Eg: LiF > KF
Cations with pseudo inert gas config (18e⁻) have more covalent character than inert gas config. Eg: CuCl > NaCl
Sidgwick & Powell; developed by Nyholm & Gillespie
107° (reduced from 109°28' due to lp-bp repulsion)
104.5° (two lone pairs reduce angle further)
Weak electrostatic attraction between partially +ve H and highly electronegative atom (F, O, N)
Between different molecules. Eg: NH₃, H₂O, HF — raises boiling point
Within same molecule. Eg: o-nitrophenol, o-hydroxy benzaldehyde — lowers boiling point
H₂O forms more H-bonds (2 per molecule) than HF (1 per molecule) → higher boiling point
Zero (linear; bond moments cancel)
Non-zero (angular; bond moments do not cancel)
1 mole of any gas = 22.4 L at STP
Temperature at which real gas behaves like ideal gas
31.1°C — max temperature at which CO₂ can be liquefied
N·s·m⁻² (Pa·s); CGS unit: poise. 1 Pa·s = 10 poise
Measures magnitude of attractive forces between molecules
Gives idea about effective size of gas molecule
If A & B are each in equilibrium with C, then A & B are in equilibrium with each other
All spontaneous processes are irreversible; entropy of system increases in spontaneous processes
Entropy of a pure, perfectly crystalline substance = 0 at absolute zero (0 K)

items.latex: G = H - TS \quad\Rightarrow \Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S
\Delta H > 0,\; \Delta S > 0 \Rightarrow \text{spontaneous when } T\Delta S > \Delta H
\Delta H < 0,\; \Delta S < 0 \Rightarrow \text{spontaneous when } |\Delta H| > T|\Delta S|

items.text: Total heat change in a reaction is same whether it occurs in one step or several steps
Heat absorbed/evolved when 1 mole of substance is formed from constituent elements in standard state
Heat evolved when 1 mole of substance is completely burnt in excess O₂. Eg: C(s)+O₂(g)→CO₂(g); ΔH = −393.5 kJ
Heat to convert 1 mole solid → gas without decomposition. Eg: Na(s)→Na(g); ΔH°=108.4 kJ/mol
Heat is liberated; ΔH < 0
Heat is absorbed; ΔH > 0
If system at equilibrium is subjected to change in concentration, T or P, equilibrium shifts to nullify the effect
Equilibrium shifts forward
Equilibrium shifts backward
Equilibrium shifts towards side with fewer moles of gas
Pressure has no effect
Favours endothermic reaction
Favours exothermic reaction
200 atm (high pressure — forward reaction has fewer moles)
725–775 K (optimum — low T favours exothermic, but too slow)
Fe (powdered)
Mo or (K₂O + Al₂O₃)
1.5–1.7 atm (high pressure not used — towers corrode)
673 K (optimum)
V₂O₅ or platinised asbestos
Proton donor. Eg: HCl, H₃PO₄, CH₃COOH
Proton acceptor. Eg: H₂O, HSO₄⁻, HCO₃⁻
Species differing by one proton. HCl/Cl⁻, H₂O/H₃O⁺
Electron pair acceptor. Eg: BF₃, AlCl₃, SiF₄, CO₂, SO₃, simple cations (Ag⁺, Fe³⁺)
Electron pair donor. Eg: NH₃, H₂O, OH⁻, CN⁻, F⁻
Formation of coordinate covalent bond between Lewis acid and Lewis base
Weak acid + its salt with strong base. Eg: CH₃COOH + CH₃COONa
Weak base + its salt with strong acid. Eg: NH₄OH + NH₄Cl

items.latex: K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n} \quad\text{(\Delta n = moles of gaseous products − reactants)}

items.text: Same element undergoes both oxidation AND reduction simultaneously (intermediate OS → products with higher and lower OS)
(1) Split into half reactions (2) Balance atoms — add H₂O for O, H⁺ for H (3) Balance charge with e⁻ (4) Multiply to equalize e⁻ (5) Add half reactions
Same as acidic but add OH⁻ and H₂O instead of H⁺
OS of P = +5
OS of S = +6
OS of Mn = +6
OS of Mn = +7
Average OS of S = +2
Average OS of Fe = +8/3 (mix of FeO + Fe₂O₃)
OS of B = +3
2-alkyl anthraquinol oxidised by air → 2-alkyl anthraquinone + H₂O₂
Sodium hexametaphosphate (Na₆P₆O₁₈). Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ sequestered by forming soluble complex
Hydrated sodium aluminium silicate (NaAlSiO₄), written as NaZ
Formed by active metals (Group 1 & 2). Eg: NaH, CaH₂
Formed by p-block elements. Eg: CH₄, NH₃, H₂O, HF
Too few electrons for Lewis structure. Lewis acids. Eg: B₂H₆ (diborane), Group 13 hydrides
Exact electrons for Lewis structure. Eg: CH₄, Group 14. Tetrahedral shape.
Excess lone pair electrons. Lewis bases. Eg: NH₃(1 lp), H₂O(2 lp), HF(3 lp)
Formed by d-block and f-block metals. Variable composition.
Releases large heat; less pollutant than petrol; used in rockets, fuel cells, welding (oxy-H₂ torch)
(1) Moderator in nuclear reactor (2) Exchange reactions to study reaction mechanism

items.latex: Li < K < Na < Rb < Cs \quad\text{(K anomalous — large atomic size)}

items.text: Highly electropositive; readily lose 1 electron → M⁺ ions
Cannot make K₂CO₃ — KHCO₃ is too soluble to be precipitated, unlike NaHCO₃
Carbon anode, mercury cathode
Anhydrous CaSO₄ formed above 393 K
Na₂CO₃·10H₂O. Loses 9H₂O on moderate heating → monohydrate. Loses all H₂O above 373K → soda ash
Suspension of Mg(OH)₂ in water — used as antacid
Manufacture of cement, sodium carbonate, sugar purification, dye stuffs
Found mainly in blood plasma. Transmits nerve signals; regulates water flow across cell membranes; transports sugars and amino acids into cells
Found mainly within cell fluids. Activates enzymes; participates in ATP production from glucose; transmits nerve signals
Cofactor for all enzymes using ATP in phosphate transfer; central atom of chlorophyll (light absorption in plants)
99% in bones and teeth; neuromuscular function; blood coagulation; regulated at ~100 mg/L in plasma by calcitonin and parathyroid hormone
+3 (stability decreases down the group due to inert pair effect)
Reluctance of ns² electrons to participate in bond formation. Due to this: Tl shows +1, Pb shows +2, Bi shows +3 as stable OS
Group 13 hydrides (Eg: B₂H₆ — diborane) act as Lewis acids due to incomplete octet
BF₃, BCl₃, AlCl₃ — Lewis acids due to incomplete octet
SiF₄, SnCl₄ — Lewis acids due to expansion of octet
Amphoteric (reacts with both acids and bases)
Substance present in nature that causes pollution
Substance not found in nature but released by human activity causing pollution
Amount of O₂ required to oxidise organic substances present in polluted water
Amount of O₂ used by microorganisms in water during 5 days at 20°C
Clean water ≈ 1 ppm | Municipal sewage ≈ 100–4000 ppm | Polluted water ≈ 17 ppm
Medium that reacts with a pollutant. Eg: Oceans are important sinks for CO₂
Permissible level of toxic pollutant in atmosphere that affects a person when exposed for 7–8 hours/day
Medium affected by a pollutant. Eg: eye irritation caused by smoke
Detection of different chemical forms of inorganic/organic compounds in the environment causing pollution
Increase in temperature of earth's surface due to CO₂, ozone, CFCs and water vapour in troposphere
(1) Melting of polar ice caps and flooding of low-lying areas (2) Increase in infectious diseases like dengue, malaria, yellow fever
SO₂ and NO₂ after oxidation and reaction with water form oxoacids → acid rain. pH < 5.6
(1) Washes away nutrients from soil (2) Respiratory ailments (3) Corrodes water pipes — leaches heavy metals like Fe (4) Damages buildings
Mixture of smoke, fog and sulphur dioxide
Ozone, nitric oxide, acrolein, formaldehyde, peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)
(1) Serious health problems (2) Corrosion of metals, stones, building materials
Peroxyacetyl nitrate — powerful eye irritant
Depletion of ozone layer. Causes skin cancer, cataract, damage to fish production
Excessive growth of algae in water bodies due to over-nutrition from agricultural/industrial waste → leads to drying up of lake
Trigonal planar, sp² hybridised
Forms carbocation (+) and carbanion (−)
Polarisation of σ bond caused by polarisation of adjacent σ bond
Polarity due to interaction of two π bonds or π bond and adjacent lone pair
Transfer of shared π electrons to one atom of a multiple bond in presence of attacking reagent
Delocalisation of σ electrons of C–H bond of alkyl group attached to unsaturated carbon
Reagent and substrate combine to give a single product
Atom or group of substrate replaced by another atom/group
Two or more atoms/groups removed from substrate to form multiple bonds
Less stable species rearranges to more stable species
Reagent with an electron pair (electron-rich species)
Electron-seeking reagent (electron-deficient species)
Used to detect N, S, halogens, P in organic compounds
154 pm | C–H bond length: 112 pm
NaOH + CaO mixture; used in decarboxylation
Alkyl halides + Na in dry ether → higher alkane. Eg: 2CH₃I + 2Na → C₂H₆ + 2NaI
Decomposition of higher alkanes → lower alkanes + alkenes on strong heating in absence of air
773 K, 10–20 atm, Cr₂O₃/V₂O₅/Mo₂O₃ catalyst
134 pm | Bond enthalpy: 384 kJ/mol
sp² (unsaturated carbon in alkenes)
Alkyl halide + alcoholic KOH → alkene. Eg: C₂H₅Br + KOH(alc.) → C₂H₄
Vicinal dihalide + Zn in alcohol → alkene. Eg: CH₂BrCH₂Br + Zn → C₂H₄
In addition of HX to unsymmetrical alkene, H adds to carbon with more H atoms (major product)
In presence of peroxides, H adds to carbon with fewer H (HBr only)
0.33 Debye (non-zero)
Zero (bond moments cancel)
Groups that direct incoming group to ortho & para positions. Eg: -OH, -NH₂, -CH₃, -X
Groups that direct incoming group to meta position. Eg: -NO₂, -COOH, -CHO, -CN
